This is a pretty good usecase to test whether it's actually necessary to have both `pyo3` and `rust-cpython` types in existence at the same time. In general, I don't think that we do: see below.

----

Taking [ensure_remote_has_recursive](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/b4febf1c392aa05269af782401366e030e38a6e3/src/rust/engine/src/externs/interface.rs#L1648-L1676) as an example: the signature takes a `PyList` containing generic `PyObject` instances, each of which we assert is either a `PyDigest` or a `PyFileDigest` instance: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/b4febf1c392aa05269af782401366e030e38a6e3/src/rust/engine/src/externs/interface.rs#L1661-L1662

Both `rust-cpython` and `pyo3` have unsafe methods that take a `PyObject` pointer:
* https://dgrunwald.github.io/rust-cpython/doc/cpython/struct.PyObject.html#method.from_owned_ptr
* https://docs.rs/pyo3/0.14.1/pyo3/prelude/struct.Py.html#method.from_owned_ptr

And that means that we can convert from the `PyObject` type of one of them to the `PyObject` type of the other using a small amount of unsafe code. Once you've converted from one `PyObject` type into the other, you can use the per-library casting methods to convert into the type that you actually wanted.

----

So: it seems like it should be fine for a `rust-cpython` method to accept types generated by `pyo3`, and vice-versa. The primary downside is that you must accept `PyObject` rather than a more specific type in the interface (but the `mypy` typestubs would still be accurate, so the majority of our type safety would be preserved).

From a different POV, I'm not really sure how I feel about the partial loads like I've mentioned before long ago. I get the limits are a thing and there needs to be some sort of solution to the problem. One of the ones I've thought about involve tagging the commands as optional or not and when installing a cog the user can pick which optional components they want to add or have it fail if the slash commands themselves can't fit within the limit. It's a middle ground that allows extension developers to pick what functionality they expect to be there without it being taken from them should they do something with it at runtime.

Anyway these are just random musings. I didn't spend much time on this review, only like 5 minutes or so.

There were a few things I noticed when manually testing: 
- I think `sign_transaction` still needs to be updated to support 1559. If I send a tx with both 1559 and legacy fields, I get a ValueError back from Geth, rather than our web3 `TransactionTypeMismatch` error
- In the same vein, if I `sign_transaction` with an int in the `maxFeePerGas` field: 
```
w3.eth.sign_transaction({
    'from': w3.eth.default_account, 
    'to': acct2, 
    'value': 100, 
    'maxFeePerGas': 3000000000,
    'nonce': w3.eth.get_transaction_count(w3.eth.default_account),
})
```
I get an `invalid argument 0: json: cannot unmarshal non-string into Go struct field TransactionArgs.maxFeePerGas of type *hexutil.Big"` error.
- It's probably also worth writing a few integration tests for `replace_transaction` and `modify_transaction` to prove to ourselves that we can handle both legacy and 1559 transaction params with those two methods. A quick look suggests that we'll at least need to add the new keys to the `VALID_TRANSACTION_PARAMS` in `web3/_utils/transactions.py` but there may be other things that need to be done as well. 
- It may be worth adding a note to the docs for the Gas Price API and/or `w3.eth.generate_gas_price` that those will only work for legacy transactions

Thanks for all of your work on this!

I think there are two things we can do better in this PR:

- We should make actor manager to do the initialization:
  -   if failed to get the serve deployment, we just call actor manager to create it (central place , no concurrence issue)
  - we don't need the user to do serve.deploy kind of thing. This should be hided from the user.

- Another thing is about the ordering:
  - register -> send signal now is the only correct thing to do. In the opposite, it'll crash. And we can't send event twice before it got consumed. I'm ok with this semantics right now given that we don't have enough use cases. But I think the documentation or error message should be improved to let the user understand this.

I can see how what you've implemented suits a case different from the chromium/djb code: For domains used for web browsing, the CAs are [not supposed to](https://github.com/ypcrts/fqdn/issues/14#issuecomment-688604160) sign certs that have an underscore in the apex domain name or public suffix (i.e. TLD).  TLS is so important in 2020 that I see the CAs agreement as almost defining what a valid domain name is now, but originally your case for this option was based off what browsers accept, not what CAs accept. As for what is valid in the real world, I think Chromium source is a good authority. Is there another conflicting authority?

If you have a use case for an option that determines whether underscores are allowed as any of the first char of a label or in the apex domain or TLD, then I'd like to see an authority for that.

I would prefer that we simply match the chromium/djb path with this option.  If you want to make that change, I'll accept it. And like you said in #14, the name `allow_underscores` suits this better, but I can port it over after merge.

Thanks for your work on this again

I have a couple of comments, most notably that we should avoid in-place operations in the input argument, I already had subtle bugs in the past because of this (imagine your results after the 1st epoch being completely wrong).

I also have a meta question that I would like to discuss here: we originally discussed about adding 2 conversion functions (`xyxy_to_xywh` and the other way around), but we also added the `cxcywh_to_xyxy` variants as well.
This brings the question of the scalability of the approach, as for each new format it adds at least 2 new functions (or ~ `2 * (n ** 2 - (n - 1) ** 2)` if we do the full conversion matrix).
For reference, [Detectron2 uses a `BoxMode`](https://github.com/facebookresearch/detectron2/blob/cb7e86f821f0bedb3da8f1e0a527dd821d8e5f7e/detectron2/structures/boxes.py#L13-L130) class to represent / implement the conversion types, which lets it handle the conversions as it wishes, with only a single entry-point.

I'm not advocating for using `BoxMode` (or something like this), but my original idea was that we would only be adding support for `xyxy` and `xywh`, which is still manageable.

I'm looking forward to your thoughts

I noticed that LLVM/clang can emit multiple sections with the same name in order to save space in the resulting binary when -ffunction-sections is used. The current code also doesn't handle this case, so using get_section_by_name() on such an object file will always return the last section with the non-unique name. See the following example:

```
$ cat two_f.c
void a(){}
void b(){}

$ clang-9 -ffunction-sections -fno-unique-section-names -c -o two_f.o two_f.c

$ readelf -S two_f.o
There are 11 section headers, starting at offset 0x208:

Section Headers:
  [Nr] Name              Type             Address           Offset
       Size              EntSize          Flags  Link  Info  Align
  [ 0]                   NULL             0000000000000000  00000000
       0000000000000000  0000000000000000           0     0     0
  [ 1] .strtab           STRTAB           0000000000000000  000001b0
       0000000000000057  0000000000000000           0     0     1
  [ 2] .text             PROGBITS         0000000000000000  00000040
       0000000000000000  0000000000000000  AX       0     0     4
  [ 3] .text             PROGBITS         0000000000000000  00000040
       0000000000000006  0000000000000000  AX       0     0     16
  [ 4] .text             PROGBITS         0000000000000000  00000050
       0000000000000006  0000000000000000  AX       0     0     16
[...]

$ python3
Python 3.6.9 (default, Jul 17 2020, 12:50:27)
[GCC 8.4.0] on linux
>>> from elftools.elf.elffile import ELFFile
>>> elf = ELFFile(open('two_f.o', 'rb'))
>>> print(elf.get_section_by_name('.text').header)
Container({'sh_name': 1, 'sh_type': 'SHT_PROGBITS', 'sh_flags': 6, 'sh_addr': 0, 'sh_offset': 80, 'sh_size': 6, 'sh_link': 0, 'sh_info': 0, 'sh_addralign': 16, 'sh_entsize': 0})
```

In the same manner, your code will always return the last index (in our example above 4) for '.text'.

I'm not sure if that's something that needs general fixing but I would like to keep that in mind.

The latest commits add the following changes:

- Following #7170, we no longer need special handling for any non-image parts of the samples, since all our datasets fit the heuristic.
- Following https://github.com/pytorch/vision/pull/7171#issuecomment-1419241405 we no longer use the `datapoints.Label` class. Together with the pass-through for PIL images, this means that this wrapper is a no-op for classification datasets. This also resolves all discussions regarding the categories, since they are no longer present.
- Instead of having a wrapper that takes the dataset as well as the sample as inputs, i.e.
  
  ```python
  def wrapper(dataset, sample):
      ...
      return wrapped_sample
  ```

  the architecture was changed to a factory pattern:

  ```python
  def wrapper_factory(dataset):
      def wrapper(sample):
          ...
          return wrapped_sample
  
      return wrapper
  ```

  In addition to making the wrapper more "natural", this also enables us to raise on unsupported behavior on wrapping rather than when the first sample is drawn. That should improve UX.
- I've added automated smoke tests to our datasets v1 tests to make sure wrapping and drawing samples doesn't raise anything.

I agree that we need a spec here. I think we're going to have to work together to come up with one. I'm pretty sure that we do eventually to port the cycle-breaking code that @t0yv0 added last year to the other SDKs. However, I think that what you have here works, and we should probably port it to the other SDKs as well.

If I had to give an _informal_ spec of the expected behavior as of today, I'd say that it's something like this. The dependencies of a resource `R` is the set of custom and remote component resources reachable through the union of `R`'s implicit and explicit dependencies (i.e. resources referred to by `R`'s inputs and those present in `R`'s `dependsOn` option, if any. A resource `A` is _reachable_ from another resource `B` if `B` is a non-remote component resource and `A` is one of its descendants.

I think that the changes in this PR agree with that informal statement.

I came across another general issue. We currently filter out all unplayable tracks and this means we'll be removing them whenever we make any playlist change, which is wrong. Tracks might be currently unplayable if unavailable in the geo region where the user is logged in (but may be playable from elsewhere and/or in the future). I think local files might also be treated as unplayable but I'm not sure if Spotify still supports this feature I never used it. I think to workaround this we'll need to change the current playlist behaviour (which has been the same since our first implementation) to now include unplayable tracks in playlists. libspotify won't be able to load these tracks when it comes to try and play them and they will be skipped with an `info` log message (which we will want to adjust). These unplayable tracks will clutter the queue/playlist listing and might be confusing to users when they try to play them. But I don't see another way to avoid losing them when we use `save()`.

I've not looked closely at the myers implementation here, I assume it's the normal one that's available everywhere. It seems odd that in 2020 there is no standard, fast (C), robust implementation available for us to just pull in and use. It's not ideal we have to reproduce (and test) our own version of this common algorithm. But we do have Python's standard `difflib.SequenceMatcher`, so what does using myers provide over just using that? I did find multiple (why???) packages for Google's [diff-match-patch](https://github.com/google/diff-match-patch) library. That seems to use many of the optimisations we originally talked about in #236 but I am indifferent to using that, seems complicated. 

At a higher level, some of what has been added to playlist.py belongs in web.py. You'll notice there isn't currently any Spotify Web API specific stuff in playlist.py. I think we want to keep that separation.

To save code, I think all of the assignments "aRxInfo.mClass = RxInfo::kPeerMessage;" could be deleted and replaced by a single one of this assignment on top where the aRxInfo instance is initialized. Since all messages not explicitly defined otherwise should be 'peer'. In case of an unknown/invalid message type this assignment can always be set again to kUnknown in the exit code section.  This should work since all of the Handle...() methods for particular MLE message types are private members and all the Handle..() methods are only called from HandleUdpReceive().

We can also consider to not process (handle) any Peer MLE messages with peerKSC > myKSC+1, see SPEC-1025 discussion.

See also individual line comments.

While it makes sense to make the code more resilient against `PermissionError`, I think it would be better to fix the root cause of the issue, namely that the `localstack/infra/...` folders within the Docker container are owned by root and not writable by other users.

Running under a non-root user currently breaks the startup/install routine, as you pointed out:
```
$ DOCKER_FLAGS='--user localstack' DEBUG=1 localstack start
...
File "/opt/code/localstack/localstack/services/install.py", line 525, in install_dynamodb_local
save_file(log4j2_file, log4j2_config)
File "/opt/code/localstack/localstack/utils/files.py", line 64, in save_file
with open(file, mode, opener=_opener if permissions else None) as f:
PermissionError: [Errno 13] Permission denied: '/opt/code/localstack/localstack/infra/dynamodb/log4j2.xml'
```

If we add the following line to our `Dockerfile`:
```
RUN chmod -R 777 localstack/infra/dynamodb
```
... then the startup works fine:
```
$ DOCKER_FLAGS='--user localstack' DEBUG=1 localstack start
...
Ready.
```

(We could apply the same chmod to the entire infra folder, not just `infra/dynamodb`)

Thanks for adding this example. I like the amount of work put into explaining the individual steps, and especially with the analogy you provide. Working with time series is always tricky, so having an example explicitly for that is great.

At the start of the notebook, could you add the buttons to run it on colab and view the source? Same as the other notebooks have.

I think that the main story of the notebook could be simplified. E.g. how important is it that we have multioutput (i.e. y=X² and y=X³)? Going to your pizza example, would the 2nd y be the number of ordered drinks? Maybe the whole message can be simplified by dropping the multioutput part, instead focusing on multi time series and the cross-validation part?

> y = np.hstack([X**2, X**3]).astype(np.float32)

I wonder if a sinusoidal shape for y wouldn't be better.

> but you will lose the per-restaurant information that day 3 may depend on day 2 and so on

This one is not quite clear to me. Could you please expand the explanation?

In general, I would assume that most users who deal with time-series would like to input past sales as a feature to predict future sales (probably in an auto-regressive fashion, but that depends). This does not appear to be the case here, as `X` does not contain any features from `y[t-1]` etc. Maybe that's not the point of the exercise, but it did leave me a bit confused.

> regressor = skorch.toy.make_regressor(input_units=1, output_units=y.shape[1], hidden_units=10)

For these types of examples, I prefer to explicitly define the module instead of using `toy`. It's just easier to understand for the user what's going on.

> timeful multi regression
> getX = lambda i: np.linspace(0+i, 10+i, 10)[None, :, None].astype(np.float32)
> X = np.vstack([getX(i) for i in range(10)])

I'm a little confused by this. Perhaps it would help if we didn't have two dimensions of size 10? One is time, the other the number of restaurants?

Also:

```python
>>> X[0, :, 0].tolist()
[0.0,
 1.1111111640930176,
 2.222222328186035,
 3.3333332538604736,
 4.44444465637207,
 5.55555534362793,
 6.666666507720947,
 7.777777671813965,
 8.88888931274414,
 10.0]
```

Not sure if this is intended to be `[0, 1, 2, ...]`. Doesn't really matter for the example but I wanted to bring it up.

And there is also a different way to create such a grid without custom functions:

```python
nx, ny = (11, 11)
x = np.linspace(0, 10, nx)
y = np.linspace(0, 10, ny)
xv, yv = np.meshgrid(x, y)
X = xv + yv
```

> assert X.shape == (10, 10, 1)

What is the purpose for the dangling dimension?

> plot_time_series

Could you please add some labels to the plots?

> plot_time_series(X, y_pred)

It would be nice to also show `y` but I guess it's not practical because of the y-axis scale.

> list(ts_splitter.split(X, y))

I think a visualization like this can be helpful:

```python
_, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 3))
for i, (xi, yi) in enumerate(ts_splitter.split(X, y)):
    ax.plot(xi, i * np.ones_like(xi), marker='o', c='c', label="train")
    ax.plot(yi, i * np.ones_like(yi), marker='o', c='b', label="valid")
    if i == 0:
        ax.legend()
ax.set_yticks([0, 1, 2])
ax.set_ylabel("split");
```

> Using the time-series split for internal validation is easy.

I have learned that one should avoid terms like "easy" to not intimidate users for whom it's not easy. So maybe something like "can be achieved in a few steps" or so.

> train_split=ValidSplit(cv=ts_splitter)

Hmm, I wonder: Since skorch takes only the first split, does it mean that a bunch of data is discarded completely, because the first ts split only goes from 0-5?

> The default scorer for regression problems in sklearn is R² ...

I feel like the whole discussion around R² is a bit of a distraction from the main point. Would it be better just not to mention it and just go straight to MSE for instance?

> cv_results

Maybe add a few sentences about what we can learn from the CV results before going straight to the conclusion?

> We saw that it is possible and easy to use RNN and skorch

Again, let's avoid "easy".

I had a look through and I think the only thing I can think that needs to change in this PR is making a hard-coded packetsize constant `static constexpr`. Otherwise, the following set of general changes is implemented: 

- `stream_base::queue_storage` changes from `storage_type` to `queue_entry`.
- `spead2::detail::storage` is a vendored `std::aligned_storage` with a cleaner API.
- `std::make_unique` used instead of `std::unique_ptr<T>(new T)`
- `std::launder` used to avoid unwanted compiler optimisations on placement new allocated types.
- Removes some callable wrapper classes in favour of c++14 lambdas
- `[[maybe_unused]]` to remove compiler warnings for unused arguments. In other places, arguments are anonymised. I wondered why there is a distinction between the two cases? Possibly for the sake of existing API calls?

This looks good overall. Regarding the ManagedArray discussion, the thing to keep in mind is that conceptually `ManagedArray` is really a low-level RAII wrapper around a pointer to a contiguous memory buffer whose ownership can be transferred. `std::vector` should be used more like a list, whereas `ManagedArray` should be like a numpy array. The expectation is that Python users acquiring `ManagedArray` variables expect that those variables should be zero-copy but remain unchanged if a recompute is triggered. Since it looks like you're replacing all the `ManagedArray` data in the vector on recompute, I think you'll be fine. I'll double-check this on my final pass though since I was a bit cursory with checking that this time around.

> Also, remote build doesn't seem to be launching the build when I tested the instructions, not sure what's going on there.

I think what's going on here is this command is really slow if you don't have a clean clone of our repo. `snapcraft` sends everything under your CWD to Launchpad and we often have things like multiple virtual environments (each of which is a ~200MB), multiple clones of the repo under `releases/` or `tests/letstest/`, etc.

You can see this by running `snapcraft remote-build` directly with a command like `snapcraft remote-build --build-on=amd64` from the root of our repo to build the Certbot snap. On a clean clone on my laptop, in about a minute it prints:
```
Packaging sources for certbot...
Packaging sources for shared-metadata...
Sending build data to Launchpad... (https://bradmwarren:<token>@git.launchpad.net/~bradmwarren/+git/snapcraft-certbot-06e0c4/)
Enumerating objects: 12, done.
Counting objects: 100% (12/12), done.
Delta compression using up to 4 threads
Compressing objects: 100% (10/10), done.
Writing objects: 100% (12/12), 59.22 MiB | 938.00 KiB/s, done.
Total 12 (delta 1), reused 0 (delta 0), pack-reused 0
To https://git.launchpad.net/~bradmwarren/+git/snapcraft-certbot-06e0c4/
 * [new branch]      HEAD -> master
If interrupted, resume with: 'snapcraft remote-build --recover'
Building snap package for amd64. This may take some time to finish.
```
In the clone of the Certbot repo I normally use it hangs on the earlier steps much longer.

Assuming this was the problem for you, I think adding a sentence about this to our docs is a good idea.

I haven't looked much at the code in particular but  I did notice that there is only one root `.gitignore` file in the eyes of `get_sources`. This is a problem since files outside of the scope (or should I say folder) of a `.gitignore` file would still be subject to its exclude rules.

Let's say we have a directory structure like this: 
```
.
├── a
│   ├── a.py
│   ├── b.py
│   └── .gitignore
├── a.py
├── b.py
├── c.py
└── .gitignore
```

And with `./.gitignore` containing `c.py` and `./a/.gitignore` containing `b.py`. 

I would expect that only `./c.py` and `./a/b.py` would be ignored by Git and Black. This is true with Git, but not with Black. Black also ignores the root level `b.py` file because the nested `.gitignore`'s contents was added to the "root" `.gitignore` that all the files and directories Black finds recursively are subject to.

```console
(black) ichard26@acer-ubuntu:~/programming/oss/black$ gh pr checkout 1750
(black) ichard26@acer-ubuntu:~/programming/testing/gitignore$ git status
On branch master
Untracked files:
  (use "git add <file>..." to include in what will be committed)
	a.py
	a/a.py
	b.py

nothing added to commit but untracked files present (use "git add" to track)
(black) ichard26@acer-ubuntu:~/programming/testing/gitignore$ black .  --check -v
.git ignored: matches the --exclude regular expression
c.py ignored: matches the .gitignore file content
b.py ignored: matches the .gitignore file content
a/b.py ignored: matches the .gitignore file content
/home/ichard26/programming/testing/gitignore/a/a.py already well formatted, good job.
/home/ichard26/programming/testing/gitignore/a.py already well formatted, good job.
All done! ✨ 🍰 ✨
2 files would be left unchanged.
```
---

Perhaps make `gen_python_files` only search through one directory and then return the non-excluded files and subdirectories it found. So similar to `os.walk`. And then on every call to `gen_python_files` in `get_sources` a "`.gitignore` file" is created that makes sense for the current directory it's searching. To make this work you would also have to keep track of the all different `.gitignore` files and their locations.

*I'm sorry if that didn't make any sense at all, I'm not great at explain ideas clearly... Anyway, it's just a suggestion to how you could fix this issue. You probably can think of something better :D Thanks for working on this!

For others interested in understanding better the motivation behind this PR, I suggest https://docs.nvidia.com/deploy/pdf/CUDA_Compatibility.pdf .  One paragraph worth repeating from that doc is:
```
To use other CUDA APIs introduced in a minor release (that require a new
driver), one would have to implement fallbacks or fail gracefully. This situation
is not different from what is available today where developers use macros to
compile out features based on CUDA versions. Users should refer to the CUDA
headers and documentation for new CUDA APIs introduced in a release.
```
Thus, it's fair to use an 11.1 feature that is supported by both 11.1 and 11.0 kernel-mode drivers.  Before using an 11.1 feature that requires an 11.1 kernel-mode driver, one should check dynamically for that feature's presence at runtime, as suggested in the document section 3.2 "Handling New CUDA Features."  This is particularly important to pay attention to while the upstream CI testing has no enhanced-compatibility build.

Will review the code, but first two quick design questions - cc @jtamiace 

## Icon buttons

It's rare in Kolibri (versus Studio) to use purely icon buttons rather than text-based so I wanted to make sure this was intentional. This is partly because learners on tablets don't have tooltips, and partly because learners are less likely to know iconography conventions in contrast to Studio where we use icon buttons more liberally for "power users".

| deletion in bookmarks | deletion in coach UI |
|--|--|
| ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2367265/134698606-066257ee-2b83-48f4-bd11-3f3f8a979e59.png) | ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2367265/134702357-551ca56c-7562-4072-8257-526335cd3bd9.png) |


If we do stick with icon buttons, note that we might be using the wrong one for 'delete':

* https://design-system.learningequality.org/icons/#token-clear
* https://design-system.learningequality.org/icons/#token-delete

## Empty states

We've made 'empty states' more consistent, and I think this design deviates slightly from previous directions:

* https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/pull/5051
* https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/pull/3550

I would have expected it to look more like:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2367265/134700995-1dd393a3-9368-4a96-a45e-e516bbf0ea02.png)

Perhaps something to track in KDS?

Thanks very much for adding tests and documenting the CSV issue in #5093.

The tests made me realize that for input data with row names, the value of `reshape` isn't sufficient to determine whether or not the output should have row names.

`reshape`'s value only matters for multi-class classification...for regression and binary classification tasks, the number of rows in the output from `predict()` should be the same as the number of rows in `newdata`, so for those objectives row names should be preserved _regardless of the value of `reshape`_.

Could you please do the following:

* move the code you've added into `predict.lgb.Booster()` into `Predictor$predict()`
* ensure that for binary classification and regression, row names are always preserved

For reference, here's the relevant code in `Predictor$predict()` that is figuring out whether or not the predictions have the same number of elements as the input data has rows.

https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/17d4e007852b4f0b34b211a674cb26e6751c98e2/R-package/R/lgb.Predictor.R#L215-L231
Thank you for fixing this!

I just had a quick look at what you have here and left a couple of notes. 
Looks pretty good already even though it is still early 👍 

Additionally I would recommend having a look at the kopf settings. I listed a couple that would make sense IMO.

```python
@kopf.on.startup()
def configure(settings: kopf.OperatorSettings, **_: Any) -> None:
    # Set server and client timeouts to reconnect from time to time.
    # In rare occasions the connection might go idle we will no longer receive any events.
    # These timeouts should help in those cases.
    # https://github.com/nolar/kopf/issues/698
    # https://github.com/nolar/kopf/issues/204
    settings.watching.server_timeout = 120
    settings.watching.client_timeout = 150
    settings.watching.connect_timeout = 5

    # The default timeout is 300s which is usually to long
    # https://kopf.readthedocs.io/en/latest/configuration/#networking-timeouts
    settings.networking.request_timeout = 10

    # With these settings you can enable leader election. Might not be
    # relevant for the moment but something to keep in mind.
    # You also need to create peering object which can be found here https://github.com/nolar/kopf/blob/main/peering.yaml
    # https://kopf.readthedocs.io/en/latest/peering/
    settings.peering.mandatory = True
    settings.peering.clusterwide = True
    
    # You will probably want to configure your own identifiers/prefixes
    # so that you don't run into any conflicts with other kopf based
    # operators in the cluster. I recommend changing the following settings:
    settings.peering.name = ""
    settings.persistence.finalizer = ""
    settings.persistence.progress_storage = kopf.AnnotationsProgressStorage(
        prefix=""
    )
    settings.persistence.diffbase_storage = kopf.AnnotationsDiffBaseStorage(
        prefix=""
    )
```

You might also want to enable the health check endpoint of kopf and configure some probes for it
https://kopf.readthedocs.io/en/latest/probing/.

I misunderstood so my fix (https://github.com/onnx/onnx/pull/4234) was incorrect --

Instead of storage type, actually make_tensor with raw directly uses its original NumPy type (defined in TENSOR_TYPE_TO_NP_TYPE) as bytes. Only TensorProto.FLOAT16 and TensorProto.BFLOAT16 are exceptions. Although now TensorProto.FLOAT16 uses uint16 as raw_data, the itemsize for uint16 and float16 is the same (2) so TensorProto.FLOAT16 is not an issue.

Thus, just like what this PR does, we only need to handle the special case TensorProto.BFLOAT16, because float32's itemsize is 4 and uint16 itemsize is 2 (there is a truncation when processing original float32 NumPy array).

Another finding is for making float16 data with raw, actually there is no need to convert original float16 NumPy array as `np_array.view(dtype=np.uint16).flatten().tobytes()` for raw_data, because their tobytes() are identical (See [here](https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/a92870cdf359297495a118184dca2eaecee4b717/onnx/test/helper_test.py#L414)). I will send another PR to improve it. Let's merge this PR first for upcoming ONNX 1.12.

Hello @hemanth-silabs 

Thank you for this contribution, it's an important step towards more secure products! Good work!

I have an overall question about architecture. Right now the code is sliced with ifdefs in many places to choose between PSA API and mbedTLS API.

Haven't you considered only using the PSA API (whenever possible)? As far as I understand, we can update the mbedTLS version to one that supports PSA API (e.g. 2.25 from TF-M 1.3) - I have it done on a branch and can contribute. Since for non-TF-M type of devices, PSA API implementation translates code to mbedTLS API under the hood anyway. For example the `psa_hash_update` will call `mbedtls_sha256_update_ret` - [code](https://github.com/ARMmbed/mbedtls/blob/v2.25.0/library/psa_crypto.c#L2655). The only caveat is that for such non-TF-M devices, we would need to implement ITS API for the key-by-reference usage.

https://github.com/scikit-hep/uproot5/blob/d2adb7cc4f9e6e196dfe119232080d30ff2fcb83/src/uproot/model.py#L788-L789

and restores them

https://github.com/scikit-hep/uproot5/blob/d2adb7cc4f9e6e196dfe119232080d30ff2fcb83/src/uproot/model.py#L873

The rest of the instances in

  - [ ] src/uproot/models/TH.py
  - [ ] src/uproot/models/TGraph.py
  - [ ] src/uproot/models/TAtt.py
  - [ ] src/uproot/behaviors/TBranch.py

were originally generated by the code (that you fixed) in src/uproot/streamers.py, but now that they're disconnected, you have to manually update them in the same way.

Can we fix the lint issues, then add the following example for non-k8s models (I've tested and it works):

```python
"""
This example:

1. Connects to the current model
2. Deploy a charm and waits until it reports itself active
3. Destroys the unit and application

"""
from juju import loop
from juju.model import Model


async def main():
    model = Model()
    print('Connecting to model')
    await model.connect()

    try:
        print('Deploying ')
        application = await model.deploy(
            'ch:juju-qa-test',
            application_name='juju-qa-test',
            channel='2.0/edge',
        )

        print('Waiting for active')
        await model.block_until(
            lambda: all(unit.workload_status == 'active'
                        for unit in application.units))

    finally:
        print('Disconnecting from model')
        await model.disconnect()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    loop.run(main())
```
